care.summary: Peace Lily Sensation is a large cultivar of Spathiphyllum, growing up to 4-5 feet tall with enormous deep green leaves. Native to tropical Americas, it's prized for its majestic foliage and elegant white spathes. One of the best low-light flowering houseplants — forgiving, dramatic, and air-purifying.
care.light: Low to medium indirect light — one of the few flowering plants that genuinely thrives in low light. Avoid direct sun. North or east-facing windows are ideal. Leaves lean toward light — rotate regularly for even growth. Survives under fluorescent/artificial office lighting.
care.water: Keep soil evenly moist but never soggy. Water when top 1-2 inches of soil feels dry — every 5-7 days in growing season, every 10-14 days in winter. Dramatic drooping when thirsty: stems and leaves collapse but perk back up within hours after watering. Use room-temperature filtered water.
care.soil: Peat-based, well-draining potting mix with good moisture retention. Mix 2 parts peat moss or coco coir + 1 part perlite + 1 part compost or worm castings. Slightly acidic pH (5.8-6.5). Commercial African violet potting mix works well. Must have drainage holes.
care.humidity: Medium to high humidity preferred (50-70%). Appreciates regular misting, pebble tray, or grouping with other plants. Tolerates average household humidity (30-40%) but brown leaf tips signal humidity too low. Bathrooms with windows are ideal.
care.fertilizer: Light feeder. Apply balanced liquid fertilizer (20-20-20) diluted to half-strength once a month during spring and summer. For more blooms, switch to phosphorus-rich (10-30-10) in early spring. Pause in fall/winter. Flush soil quarterly to prevent salt buildup.
care.toxicity: Toxic to humans and pets (cats/dogs). Contains calcium oxalate crystals causing mouth/throat burning, drooling, difficulty swallowing. Keep out of reach. Not fatal but unpleasant — contact poison control if ingested. Wear gloves when pruning.
common_issues: Drooping stems/leaves - needs water; recovers within hours but repeated wilting stresses the plant
Brown leaf tips/edges - low humidity, tap water chemicals (fluoride), or over-fertilizing; use filtered water, increase humidity
Yellow lower leaves - overwatering or poor drainage; let soil dry more between waterings
No blooms / green spathes - insufficient light; move to brighter indirect location
Black/dark leaves - cold draft or direct sun exposure; move away from cold windows/AC vents
Spider mites (fine webbing) - dry air; increase humidity, treat with insecticidal soap
Root rot (mushy roots, foul smell) - overwatering; trim rot, repot in fresh soil
Mealybugs (white fuzzy spots) - dab with rubbing alcohol, isolate, treat with soap
tips: Use the dramatic droop-recovery cycle as your watering cue — Peace Lily visibly tells you when it needs water
Wipe large leaves monthly with a damp cloth to maximize photosynthesis and maintain glossy sheen
Remove spent flower stalks at the base to encourage new blooms and keep the plant tidy
Use filtered/distilled/rainwater to prevent brown tips — Peace Lily is sensitive to fluoride and chlorine
Repot in spring when roots circle the pot; go up only 1 pot size to avoid excess moisture
Propagate by division when repotting — separate root ball into clumps of 3-5 stems
Mist leaves daily in dry environments, especially winter when indoor heating dries the air
Sensation grows much larger than standard Peace Lily — plan for a 3-5 ft tall floor plant
